title: No Tax Dollars for Terrorists Act
short_title: No Tax Dollars for Terrorists Act
stage_label: Committee on Foreign Relations. Reported by Senator Risch with an amendment in the nature of a substitute. Without writt
plain_summary: This bill requires the US government to create a plan to stop other countries and organizations from giving money or supplies to the Taliban in Afghanistan. The government must write reports about who is helping the Taliban and how to stop them from getting this support. The bill also says the US should use its own foreign aid programs to convince other countries not to help the Taliban. Additionally, the government must report on US cash assistance programs in Afghanistan and information about Afghanistan's central bank.
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 330.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the No Tax Dollars for Terrorists Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Strategy to Oppose Foreign Assistance by Foreign Countries and Nongovernmental Organizations to the Taliban
sections.text: The United States government has an official policy to stop other countries and nonprofit groups from giving money or aid to the Taliban. This is especially true for countries and groups that get help from the United States. The government will also review whether to continue giving aid to any countries or groups that have helped the Taliban. Within one hundred eighty days after this law passes, the Secretary of State must give Congress a report. This report must identify countries and groups that have given aid to the Taliban. For each one, the report must show how much aid the United States gives them, how much they gave to the Taliban, and how the Taliban used that aid. The report must also describe what the United States has done since August two thousand twenty one to stop others from helping the Taliban. The Secretary of State must create and use a plan to discourage countries and groups from helping the Taliban within one hundred eighty days. This plan must include ways to help Afghan women and girls who are suffering under Taliban rules, but it must not help the Taliban. The Secretary must give Congress a report explaining this plan when it is finished. Then every one hundred eighty days for five years, the Secretary must report on how well the plan is working to stop support for the Taliban. Within thirty days after this law passes, the Secretary must also report on why the government stopped offering reward money for capturing Sirajuddin Haqqani and other members of the Haqqani Network. This report must explain the current status of rewards for these people and any changes since September first two thousand twenty one. It must identify which Haqqani Network members are listed as global terrorists and whether the network is still called a terrorist organization. The report must describe any meetings between the United States government and these individuals since September first two thousand twenty one. It must also say if there is new information about the Haqqani Network attacking American military or civilians. This report can be public but may have secret parts. The Secretary of State must immediately stop all aid to any country or group that has helped the Taliban.

sections.title: Section 3 Report on Direct Cash Assistance Programs in Afghanistan
sections.text: Within ninety days after this law passes, the Secretary of State must give Congress a report about direct cash assistance programs in Afghanistan. This report must cover programs that the United States government paid for between August first two thousand twenty one and thirty days after this law becomes effective. The report must describe what types of partners and people received the money. It must explain how payments were made and where money was exchanged into different currencies. The report must describe how hawala systems were used. A hawala is a system of moving money through a network of money lending brokers. The report must explain what oversight exists for using hawalas to transfer money. The report must hide all personal information about individuals or groups that received cash assistance from the United States government. The report must also describe how oversight is done and how the State Department prevents the Taliban from getting this cash assistance.

sections.title: Section 4 Report on Status of Afghan Fund
sections.text: Within ninety days after this law passes, and then every one hundred eighty days for five years, the Secretary of State and Secretary of the Treasury must work together to give Congress a report on the Afghan Fund. This report must list Taliban members who work at Da Afghanistan Bank or serve on its board. It must describe how much influence the Taliban has over Da Afghanistan Bank. The report must explain the Afghan Fund's board of trustees, including how they were checked and chosen, and which United States agencies were involved in this process. It must describe what conditions must be met before money from the Afghan Fund can be given to Da Afghanistan Bank. The report must explain how the board of trustees will decide what activities are appropriate for the Fund, what information will guide their decisions, and how they will collect and verify this information. It must also describe what controls exist to make sure money is not stolen or misused by the Taliban or other bad actors when the Fund starts giving out money. All leftover money that was set aside for rebuilding Afghanistan must be taken back from various funds and programs including the Afghanistan Security Forces Fund, Economic Support Fund, International Narcotics Control and Law Enforcement, and many others. This money must be put into the general Treasury fund and used to reduce the national debt.

sections.title: Section 5 Sense of Congress Opposing Activities that Support the Taliban or Normalize Diplomatic Relations with the Taliban
sections.text: Congress believes the United States should not have normal diplomatic relations with the Taliban unless they meet certain conditions. The Taliban must work with the United States to remove al-Qaeda and other terrorist groups from Afghanistan. They must stop taking American citizens as hostages and stop wrongly detaining or persecuting Afghans who worked for the United States, served in the previous Afghan government or security forces, or supported good government and human rights including rights for women, girls, and minorities. The Taliban must cancel all rules that prevent women and girls from getting education, jobs, freedom of movement, and free speech, and must actively support these rights. They must also cancel all rules that limit the rights of ethnic, religious, and other minority groups in Afghanistan, including Hazara communities, and must actively support and respect these rights.

sections.title: Section 6 Appropriate Congressional Committees Defined
sections.text: In this law, the term appropriate congressional committees means the Committee on Foreign Affairs and the Committee on Appropriations in the House of Representatives, and the Committee on Foreign Relations and the Committee on Appropriations in the Senate.
